# Executive Summary {-}

## Key Takeaways {-}

- This working paper summarises analyses completed in the
  third year (2025/26) of the ?aayaaqa (Herring) Spawn
  Dynamics project.

- Computer simulations were used to test whether a proposed
  harvest rule could safely re-open the WCVI herring
  fishery. The rule works by estimating how many herring
  are present, ensuring enough are left to spawn (a
  "minimum escapement" level), and only allowing fishing
  on the surplus above that level.

- This rule was tested under three different views of how
  herring populations work, ranging from the standard DFO
  assessment model to models that include predation by
  Humpback Whales, sea lions, seals, and Pacific Hake.
  Including predation reduces the expected herring
  population size substantially.

- **When predation is not included** (current DFO approach),
  the harvest rule performs well: the population stays
  above the limit reference point 96\% of the time
  (exceeding the 75\% conservation objective), and
  average catches are approximately 1,900 t per year.

- **When predation is included**, the expected herring
  population is smaller. Under the single-stock predation
  model, the fishery remains open most years but catches
  are reduced. Under the three-stock predation model,
  which also adds spatial structure, the population is
  near or below the minimum escapement level and the
  fishery is closed most of the time.

- **The method used to divide the catch among Stat Areas
  (23, 24, and 25) makes very little difference** to
  outcomes under any of the models tested.

- The key finding is that whether the fishery can re-open
  depends primarily on assumptions about predation, not
  on how the catch is distributed among areas. Resolving
  uncertainty about predation mortality is the most
  important next step for WCVI herring management.

## Summary {-}

WCVI herring commercial fisheries have been closed since
2006. Before any re-opening can be considered, a harvest
rule must be developed and tested to ensure it meets
conservation objectives under a range of assumptions
about how the population works. In Phase 3 of the
?aayaaqa (Herring) Spawn Dynamics project, a
management strategy evaluation (MSE) framework was
built to do this testing.

The proposed harvest rule sets a minimum escapement
level --- a biomass threshold below which the fishery is
closed. When the estimated population exceeds this
threshold, the surplus is available for harvest up to a
maximum rate of 8.47\%. This rule was tested using
computer simulations driven by three population models
that differ in their treatment of natural mortality:
(i) the standard Fisheries and Oceans, Canada (DFO) model 
with density dependent natural mortality (*SISCAH-DDM*), 
(ii) a single-stock model that includes predation by Humpback
Whales, Harbour Seals, Steller Sea Lions, and Pacific
Hake (*SISCAH-1S-Pred*), and (iii) a three-stock model 
with the same predators and spatial structure across Statistical 
Areas 23, 24, and 25 (*SISCAH-3S-Pred*). Three methods for 
distributing catch among Stat Areas were also evaluated.

Under the standard DFO model, the harvest rule performs
well: the population remains well above conservation
thresholds and average catches are approximately
1,900 t per year. When predation is included in a
single-stock model, catches are reduced but the fishery
remains open most years. When both predation and spatial
structure are included (three-stock model), the
long-term population size is much lower, leading to
fishery closures in most years because unfished biomass
is already at or below the minimum escapement level.
The method used to distribute catch among Stat Areas has
negligible effect on outcomes under all models tested.

These results show that the proposed harvest rule is
conservative: even if the standard DFO model is wrong
about mortality and biomass returns to lower levels in
the long term, the rule protects the stock by closing
the fishery when biomass drops below the minimum
escapement level. The feasibility of a re-opening
depends primarily on assumptions about predation
mortality, not on how catch is distributed among areas.
Reducing uncertainty about predation through
continued predator monitoring, diet sampling, and
bio-energetic modelling may be more influential on
WCVI herring re-opening procedure development than 
other avenues.
